refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating mission context into performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing 'method,' 'document,' or 'history' which are design choices.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints from stakeholders), the system produces a traceable set of performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy those inputs. Verify traceability from worksite inputs to derived requirements.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall capture worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from captured worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to source mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a mission-context capture module that accepts worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental conditions) and applies a deterministic mapping function to derive performance targets; maintain a versioned baseline registry with change history and traceability links.

DP-B: Establish a parameterized performance-target template library indexed by worksite class/mission type; populate templates via structured intake questionnaire; store all instantiations with audit trail and calibration metadata.

DP-C: Deploy a real-time mission-context listener that subscribes to worksite operational data streams (policies, safety updates, environmental sensors); trigger performance-target recalculation on context change; log all derivations with source attribution and timestamp.

DP-D: Create a hybrid approach combining static mission-context intake (DP-B style) with periodic review checkpoints; at each checkpoint, re-derive targets from current worksite state; maintain dual-track history (baseline vs. current) with explicit reconciliation records.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid mission-context intake with periodic re-derivation and dual-track history. Rationale: Balances the need for documented, traceable performance targets (DP-B foundation) with adaptive responsiveness to worksite changes (DP-C capability). Avoids continuous streaming overhead (DP-C risk) while preventing stale targets (DP-B risk). Explicit dual-track history (baseline vs. current) directly satisfies acceptance criterion for 'history of baselines/calibration updates.' Checkpoint-driven re-derivation allows controlled, auditable updates aligned with worksite safety and operational governance cycles.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between mission-context intake module and performance-target derivation logic: mitigate by defining a clear, versioned schema for mission-context inputs and a separate, testable derivation function.
Dependency on worksite operational-policy data freshness: mitigate by establishing explicit data-ownership boundaries and SLAs for policy updates; do not embed policy logic in derivation module.
History/audit trail storage coupled to derivation logic: mitigate by separating derivation (stateless) from persistence (versioned store with immutable records).
Checkpoint scheduling coupled to external governance cycles: mitigate by making checkpoint timing a configurable parameter, not hardcoded; allow worksite to define review cadence.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D avoids the tight real-time coupling of DP-C (streaming listener) and the inflexibility of DP-A (single deterministic mapping). Dual-track history introduces a minor complexity (reconciliation logic) but is essential for traceability and supports independent evolution of baseline vs. current targets. Recommend separating concerns: (1) mission-context schema/intake, (2) derivation algorithm, (3) versioned storage, (4) checkpoint orchestration.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is stable enough to define meaningful checkpoints (e.g., quarterly, post-incident, policy-change events). RISK: If context changes continuously or unpredictably, checkpoint-driven approach may miss critical updates. Mitigation: Define escalation path for out-of-cycle re-derivation triggered by safety events.

ASSUMPTION: A deterministic or semi-deterministic mapping from mission context to performance targets exists and can be documented. RISK: If mapping is highly subjective or requires expert judgment, traceability and reproducibility suffer. Mitigation: Establish a decision-record template for each target derivation; include rationale, assumptions, and sign-off.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formally documented and version-controlled. RISK: If policies are informal or scattered, intake process becomes error-prone. Mitigation: Require worksite to provide a single, authoritative policy document as input; version it alongside performance targets.

ASSUMPTION: Dual-track history (baseline vs. current) can be reconciled without ambiguity. RISK: If reconciliation rules are unclear, audit trail becomes confusing. Mitigation: Define explicit reconciliation protocol (e.g., 'current supersedes baseline only after formal approval') and log all reconciliation decisions.

ASSUMPTION: Performance targets derived from mission context are independent of real-time operational data. RISK: If targets must adapt to live sensor data or dynamic conditions, DP-D may be insufficient. Mitigation: Clarify scope: is re-derivation purely policy-driven (DP-D) or does it include sensor feedback? If latter, consider hybrid with lightweight sensor-triggered alerts.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR-NGHE-005 to explicitly distinguish between (1) baseline performance targets derived from static mission context (worksite safety plan, operational policies) and (2) adaptive performance targets that respond to dynamic operational conditions. If both are required, clarify the governance model: e.g., 'baseline targets are re-derived at quarterly checkpoints; adaptive targets are updated in real-time but must remain within ±X% of baseline.' This will inform whether DP-D alone suffices or a hybrid with real-time feedback is needed. Additionally, specify the acceptance criteria for 'documented set of performance targets': does this mean a single authoritative document, a versioned registry, or a queryable database? This will guide IM (Implementation Module) design for storage and access patterns.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for mission-context intake, baseline derivation, periodic re-derivation checkpoints, dual-track history management (baseline vs. current), and auditable update governance. Owns the logic for adaptive mission target calibration while maintaining documented traceability and safety compliance.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite sensor data, environmental conditions, and operational state to inform mission-context re-derivation decisions and trigger checkpoint evaluation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs updated mission targets, performance baselines, and re-derived acceptance criteria to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraint updates and worksite governance signals; emits alerts when re-derivation results in safety-critical baseline changes requiring operator acknowledgment.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits dual-track history (baseline vs. current) logs and re-derivation audit trails for remote monitoring and compliance verification; receives remote governance/checkpoint override signals.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Mission-context intake, baseline derivation, periodic re-derivation logic, and dual-track history are inherently safety-governance concerns. Checkpoint-driven updates align with Safety Services' audit and compliance mandate. Minimal diffusion: Telemetry input and Tasking output are well-defined external interfaces; history logging stays within module. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must consume updated targets, but this is expected downstream consumption, not logic leakage.

Moderate fit if NGHE is interpreted as the overarching system orchestrator. However, DP-D's emphasis on safety governance, auditable checkpoints, and compliance-driven re-derivation suggests this logic belongs in a dedicated safety module rather than a general solution layer. Diffusion risk: mission-context intake and history management would blur NGHE's architectural clarity; re-derivation governance would scatter across multiple subsystems.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a governance or decision-making layer. Placing mission-context intake and baseline re-derivation logic in Vehicle would couple safety decisions to hardware, violate separation of concerns, and make auditing/compliance difficult. High diffusion: baseline history, checkpoint logic, and re-derivation governance would need to diffuse to Safety Services and Teleoperation anyway.

Weak fit. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command execution layer, not a mission-context or baseline governance layer. While Teleoperation may consume updated targets and display history, it should not own the logic for deriving or re-deriving baselines. High diffusion: mission-context intake, re-derivation checkpoints, and dual-track history management would need to be coordinated with Safety Services, creating tight coupling and unclear ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP-D. The DP's core logic—periodic re-derivation, checkpoint-driven updates, dual-track history, and auditable governance—are fundamentally safety and compliance concerns. Safety Services already owns the mandate for worksite safety constraints, governance cycles, and audit trails. Placing DP-D here centralizes mission-context adaptation logic in a single, well-scoped module with clear ownership, minimizes diffusion to Vehicle and Teleoperation (which consume outputs via Tasking), and ensures that baseline changes are always subject to safety review and compliance verification. The dual-track history (baseline vs. current) becomes a native artifact of Safety Services' audit responsibility.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services is not yet architected to handle real-time telemetry ingestion and rapid re-derivation cycles, latency in checkpoint evaluation could delay mission-context updates. Mitigation: Ensure Safety Services has sufficient computational capacity and event-driven architecture to process Telemetry at required frequency.

Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation have legacy dependencies on static baseline assumptions, they may not gracefully handle dynamic re-derivation outputs. Mitigation: Establish clear Tasking interface contract specifying baseline versioning and change notification semantics.
Risk: Dual-track history (baseline vs. current) could grow unbounded if not pruned. Mitigation: Define retention policies and archival strategies within Safety Services to manage history lifecycle.

Risk: Remote governance signals (via Network Link) could introduce race conditions if re-derivation checkpoints and remote overrides collide. Mitigation: Implement explicit state machine and locking semantics for checkpoint transitions.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria explicitly require 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs' and 'history of baselines/calibration updates.' Safety Services module boundary directly owns dual-track history management and auditable re-derivation checkpoints, making both criteria verifiable through module interface inspection and audit log inspection. Traceability chain (Telemetry → mission-context intake → baseline derivation → Tasking output) is architecturally explicit.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR-CR-NGHE-005 maps unambiguously to Safety Services IM boundary. The module owns mission-context intake logic, periodic re-derivation governance, checkpoint-driven updates, and dual-track history—all directly required by the FR. Downstream consumption by Vehicle and Teleoperation via Tasking interface is a well-defined external dependency, not an allocation ambiguity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (Hybrid mission-context intake with periodic re-derivation and dual-track history) directly and sufficiently addresses FR-CR-NGHE-005. The DP explicitly balances documented traceability (DP-B foundation) with adaptive responsiveness (DP-C capability), avoiding both stale targets and continuous streaming overhead. Dual-track history (baseline vs. current) and checkpoint-driven re-derivation are concrete mechanisms that satisfy the acceptance criteria for 'history of baselines/calibration updates' and 'traceable to worksite inputs.' Rationale is sound and risk-aware.

diffusion_risks: Latency risk: If Safety Services lacks real-time telemetry ingestion capacity, checkpoint evaluation could lag behind worksite state changes, delaying mission-context updates. Mitigation: Verify Safety Services has event-driven architecture and sufficient computational budget for Telemetry processing at required frequency.

Interface contract risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation may have legacy assumptions about static baselines. Dynamic re-derivation outputs could break downstream logic if Tasking interface does not clearly specify baseline versioning, change notification semantics, and backward compatibility. Mitigation: Establish explicit Tasking interface specification with version negotiation and change-event semantics.

History unbounded growth risk: Dual-track history (baseline vs. current) could accumulate without bounds, consuming storage and degrading audit query performance. Mitigation: Define and enforce retention policies, archival strategies, and pruning schedules within Safety Services.

Remote governance race condition risk: Network Link can deliver remote checkpoint overrides while Safety Services is evaluating re-derivation. Concurrent state transitions could corrupt baseline state or create audit gaps. Mitigation: Implement explicit state machine with atomic transitions and locking semantics for checkpoint state; document override precedence rules.

Moderate diffusion in downstream consumption: Vehicle and Teleoperation must consume and apply updated mission targets from Tasking. This is expected and acceptable (external interface consumption), but requires clear contracts and change propagation semantics to avoid silent failures or inconsistent state.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the correct and cohesive IM boundary for DP-D. Mission-context intake, baseline derivation, periodic re-derivation logic, dual-track history management, and auditable checkpoint governance are inherently safety and compliance concerns—core to Safety Services' mandate. Telemetry input and Tasking output are well-defined external interfaces with clear directionality. History logging and audit trails remain within the module. The only minor diffusion is expected downstream consumption by Vehicle and Teleoperation, which is architectural by design, not logic leakage. Alternative placements (NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation) would scatter safety governance logic and violate separation of concerns.

recommended_revisions: Specify Safety Services' computational and latency budget for Telemetry ingestion and checkpoint evaluation. Ensure real-time responsiveness aligns with worksite operational cycles (e.g., checkpoint frequency, re-derivation latency SLA).

Formalize Tasking interface contract: define baseline versioning scheme, change notification semantics (e.g., version increment, change-event payload), and backward compatibility guarantees. Specify how Vehicle and Teleoperation detect and handle baseline updates.
Define dual-track history lifecycle policy: retention period, archival strategy, pruning triggers, and storage quota. Specify audit log format and query interface for compliance verification.

Document Safety Services' state machine for checkpoint transitions: define states (e.g., baseline-locked, re-derivation-in-progress, baseline-updated, override-pending), transitions, atomic operations, and locking semantics. Specify precedence rules for remote overrides vs. local re-derivation.

Establish SafetyAlert interface semantics: define alert triggers (e.g., safety-critical baseline changes), acknowledgment requirements, and escalation paths. Specify how operator acknowledgment is recorded in audit trail.

Add acceptance test criteria: (1) Verify dual-track history is populated and auditable for each re-derivation checkpoint. (2) Verify Tasking outputs are traceable to Telemetry inputs and baseline derivation logic. (3) Verify remote governance signals do not corrupt baseline state or create audit gaps. (4) Verify history retention policies are enforced and storage quota is respected.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence and excellent containment (0.92 score). The DP is sound, the IM boundary is correct, and the acceptance criteria are verifiable. However, the recommended_revisions identify five critical architectural contracts and state-machine specifications that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to ensure implementation fidelity. High safety criticality demands that latency budgets, interface versioning, history lifecycle, checkpoint state machines, and audit semantics be treated as first-class functional requirements, not post-hoc implementation details. Decomposing these into sub-FRs ensures they are architecturally owned, testable, and traceable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-CR-NGHE-005.1: Establish and enforce real-time computational and latency budget for Safety Services' Telemetry ingestion and checkpoint evaluation, ensuring re-derivation latency does not exceed worksite operational cycle requirements.

FR-CR-NGHE-005.2: Define and formalize Tasking interface contract, including baseline versioning scheme, change notification semantics (version increment, change-event payload), and backward compatibility guarantees for downstream Vehicle and Teleoperation consumers.

FR-CR-NGHE-005.3: Implement dual-track history lifecycle governance, including retention period policy, archival strategy, pruning triggers, storage quota enforcement, and audit log query interface for compliance verification.

FR-CR-NGHE-005.4: Establish Safety Services checkpoint state machine with explicit state definitions (baseline-locked, re-derivation-in-progress, baseline-updated, override-pending), atomic transitions, locking semantics, and documented precedence rules for remote governance overrides vs. local re-derivation.

FR-CR-NGHE-005.5: Define SafetyAlert interface semantics, including alert triggers for safety-critical baseline changes, operator acknowledgment requirements, escalation paths, and audit trail recording of acknowledgments.

FR-CR-NGHE-005.6: Establish acceptance test criteria and verification procedures for dual-track history auditability, Tasking output traceability, remote governance state integrity, and history retention policy enforcement.

assumptions: Safety Services has or can acquire event-driven architecture capability for real-time Telemetry ingestion.
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can be updated to consume versioned baseline outputs and handle change notifications.
Remote governance (Network Link) override semantics are well-defined and do not conflict with local re-derivation logic.
Audit trail infrastructure (logging, query, retention) is available or can be provisioned within Safety Services.
Worksite operational cycle requirements (checkpoint frequency, re-derivation latency SLA) are known and stable.
open_risks: Latency risk: Safety Services checkpoint evaluation could lag behind worksite state changes if real-time ingestion capacity is insufficient. Mitigation: Sub-FR-005.1 explicitly budgets and verifies latency.

Interface contract risk: Legacy assumptions in Vehicle and Teleoperation about static baselines could break with dynamic re-derivation. Mitigation: Sub-FR-005.2 formalizes versioning and change semantics.
History unbounded growth: Dual-track history could consume unbounded storage and degrade audit performance. Mitigation: Sub-FR-005.3 enforces lifecycle and retention policies.

Remote governance race condition: Concurrent checkpoint overrides and local re-derivation could corrupt baseline state or create audit gaps. Mitigation: Sub-FR-005.4 establishes atomic state machine with explicit locking.
SafetyAlert propagation: Operator acknowledgment of safety-critical baseline changes may not be reliably recorded or escalated. Mitigation: Sub-FR-005.5 defines alert semantics and audit recording.

Verification gap: Acceptance criteria for history auditability, traceability, and state integrity may not be testable without explicit test procedures. Mitigation: Sub-FR-005.6 establishes acceptance test criteria.
evidence_needed: Safety Services computational budget and latency profile for Telemetry ingestion and checkpoint evaluation (Sub-FR-005.1).
Tasking interface specification document with baseline versioning scheme, change-event payload schema, and backward compatibility matrix (Sub-FR-005.2).
Dual-track history lifecycle policy document: retention period, archival triggers, pruning schedule, storage quota, and audit log query API (Sub-FR-005.3).
Safety Services state machine diagram with state definitions, transitions, atomic operations, locking semantics, and remote override precedence rules (Sub-FR-005.4).
SafetyAlert interface specification: alert trigger conditions, acknowledgment protocol, escalation rules, and audit trail format (Sub-FR-005.5).
Acceptance test plan with test cases for history auditability, Tasking output traceability, remote governance state integrity, and retention policy enforcement (Sub-FR-005.6).
